one_line_takeaway: Multi-agent 'sycophancy' (peer-consensus-induced correct-to-wrong answer flips) is a pretrained, attention-driven, mid-layer (L14-L18) feature-suppression vulnerability present in base models before RLHF -- not an RLHF artifact -- so pipeline-level structural defenses like injecting a single correctly-arguing dissenting agent generalize across attack framings where prompt-level alignment defenses do not.
key_finding: Under named peer jury (strong) pressure the suffixed yield is 75.75% [71.75,80.00]; assistant-role and tool-role framing of identical content saturate near ceiling at 97.75% and 98.00%. Activation patching localizes all corruption to L14-L18 (clean-to-pressured P(correct) gap = 0.764; patching at L>=18 restores 96.8% of the gap; onset discrete between L12 and L14 with non-overlapping CIs); attention carries the causal weight while MLP contribution is below detection threshold (|delta|<0.017) at every layer in the window. Base pretrained models match or exceed Instruct yield in 10/12 family x condition cells (e.g., Qwen assistant-role yield: 92.0% base vs 37.9% Instruct), indicating RLHF partially mitigates rather than causes the vulnerability -- directly refuting the paper's stated prior attribution (Wynn and Hadfield, 2025) that this is an RLHF-induced sycophancy artifact. The attack surface decomposes into channel framing (sets the evidence-demand threshold: user-role needs near-unanimity, i.e., cliffs at 4v0/80.25%, while assistant-role cliffs at majority 3v1/60.25%) crossed with consensus strength, producing a 47.5pp gap at the 3v1 transition, preserved at N=5 and N=6. SAE clamping of top-100 pressure-changed features at L19 drops P(wrong_target) by -21.9pp (falling-features-only: -15.6pp; rising-only: ~0) with only partial P(correct) restoration (+3.5pp), and a DIM direction at L25 drops P(wrong_target) by -32.5pp with +10.5pp restoration -- both consistent with pressure suppressing clean-reasoning features rather than activating a dedicated sycophancy circuit, with freed probability mass flowing to other wrong answers rather than back to correct. A single correctly-arguing dissenter reduces yield by 70.5pp (user-role: 75.75%->5.25%), 73.25pp (assistant-role: 97.75%->24.50%), and 53.5pp (tool-role: 97.75%->44.25%), and this effect survives the suffixed/unsuffixed ablation and three adaptive-attacker strategies (yield stays below 21% in user-role framing), whereas the strongest system-prompt defense drops from -65pp on its designed attack to -14 to -28pp on out-of-distribution attack variants and has near-zero effect under the unsuffixed protocol.
core_contribution: The paper mechanistically studies the 'Correct-to-Incorrect Flip' (termed 'yield'): the rate at which an LLM subject model, embedded in a multi-agent pipeline, abandons a factually correct MMLU-humanities answer after being shown peer-model outputs asserting a wrong answer. Using activation patching, logit lens, linear probes, sparse autoencoders (SAE), and difference-in-means (DIM) direction analysis on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (with Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 as replication and Gemma-2-9B/Qwen2.5-7B deferred to an appendix), the authors (1) causally localize the corruption to an attention-dominant, MLP-null mid-layer window L14-L18, where patching at L>=18 restores 96.8% of the clean-to-pressured P(correct) gap; (2) test and largely refute the dominant published attribution of this vulnerability to RLHF-induced sycophancy by showing pretrained BASE models across four families (Llama, Mistral, Gemma, Qwen) exhibit the same or higher yield than their Instruct counterparts in 10/12 matched family x condition cells; (3) decompose the attack surface into two independent factors -- channel framing (which chat-template role delivers jury content: user/assistant/tool) and consensus strength (fraction of peer agents asserting the wrong answer) -- whose interaction produces a 47.5 percentage-point yield gap at the 3-of-4-wrong (3v1) consensus point, a structure preserved at jury sizes N=4,5,6; and (4) show that a single correctly-arguing dissenting agent reduces yield by 54-73 percentage points across all three framings, generalizing better than the strongest system-prompt defense, which drops yield by 65pp on its designed attack but degrades to only -14 to -28pp outside its design surface.
threat_model: The setting is a benign-looking but adversarial multi-agent LLM pipeline: a subject model answers a factual multiple-choice question after being shown pre-generated responses from three (or four, in the count sweep) named peer/jury models that unanimously (or partially) assert a pre-committed wrong answer with persuasive supporting arguments, delivered via one of three chat-template channels (user turn, assistant turn simulating the model's own prior output, or a simulated tool/ipython-role response). This models a single compromised, adversarial, or simply wrong peer agent (or a bare declarative consensus assertion) in a production agentic pipeline -- debate-based verifiers, tool-routing systems aggregating multi-provider responses, or cost-driven small-model agent swarms. No gradient-based, white-box, or token-level jailbreak attack is used; the 'attack' is entirely at the level of natural-language jury content and its chat-role placement, i.e., a prompt-injection-adjacent, framing-based influence attack on agent consensus, not a classic security exploit against model weights or infrastructure.
limitations: The paper's own stated scope limits (Appendix F is referenced but not read in this pass; limitations inferred from main text): mechanistic analysis is centered on a single subject model (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct), with Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 used only as a within-family mechanistic replication and Gemma-2-9B/Qwen2.5-7B deferred to an appendix for behavioral-only comparison, so the depth of mechanistic claims (L14-L18 circuit, attention-vs-MLP decomposition) rests on two model families, not four. The task domain is restricted to MMLU-humanities multiple-choice factual QA (with a STEM pool and MMLU college-CS used only for onset/restoration replication, per the text), so generalization to open-ended generation, iterative multi-turn debate dynamics, or non-factual (opinion/preference) sycophancy is explicitly left to 'future work' by the authors' own Conclusion. Jury responses are pre-generated and static rather than produced through live iterative debate, which the authors state isolates the phenomenon from debate-dynamics confounds but also means the setup does not capture adaptive, iterative peer pressure. SAE feature-family validation rates are themselves partial and reported honestly: baseline-reasoning features validate at only 7/9 under minimal synthetic stimuli, consensus-signal features at 1/4, named-attribution features at 1/5, and channel-framing features at 3/5, indicating substantial uncertainty in the SAE-based feature interpretation despite the headline suppression conclusion. The dissenter-rescue mitigation is validated only against three adaptive-attacker strategies tested in user-role framing, not a fully adversarial red-team.

datasets_used: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) humanities subset: 400 questions across US history, world history, government, and philosophy, filtered to clean-prompt P(correct)>0.8
200-question STEM pool (used for cross-domain replication of the patching onset/restoration window)
MMLU college computer science (43 questions, used for cross-domain replication)
quantitative_results: Assistant-role jury (strong) yield: 97.75% [96.25, 99.00]; Tool-role jury (strong) yield: 98.00% [96.50, 99.25]
Direct user assertion yield: 44.00%; user assertion (peer-jury length, token-matched control): 45.50%
Anonymous perspectives (strong, no consensus line): 35.75%, vs Anonymous jury (strong, with consensus line): 81.00%

Activation patching: clean-to-pressured P(correct) gap = 0.764; patching at L>=18 restores 96.8% of the gap; restoration at L14 = +0.289, at L16 = +0.668; MLP component |delta|<0.017 (below detection) at every layer in L14-L18
Base vs Instruct: base models yield >= Instruct in 10/12 family x condition cells; Qwen assistant-role: 92.0% (base) vs 37.9% (Instruct)
47.5 percentage-point cross-framing yield gap at the 3v1 (3-of-4 wrong) consensus point; preserved across jury sizes N in {4,5,6}
SAE feature clamping (top-100 pressure-changed features at L19): P(wrong_target) -21.9pp (falling-only -15.6pp, rising-only ~0); P(correct) restoration only +3.5pp
DIM direction subtraction at L25, alpha=4: P(wrong_target) -32.5pp; P(correct) restoration +10.5pp
Strongest system-prompt defense: -65pp on designed attack, degrading to -28pp (bare assertion within jury block) and -14pp (no jury) outside its design surface

Clean-trained linear probe accuracy at L32 drops below 25% chance floor under pressure: 18.75% (named peer jury) and 1.50% (assistant-role jury), indicating directional substitution rather than mere degradation
baselines_compared: Direct user assertion (no jury, single aggressive user-turn assertion)
User assertion padded to peer-jury token length (rules out context-length confound)
Anonymous perspectives / anonymous jury (strips named-model attribution and/or consensus closing line, to decompose named-attribution vs consensus-assertion effects)
Weak-reasoning jury variants (deliberately nonsensical arguments) vs strong-reasoning jury variants, per channel framing
Pretrained base models (Llama, Mistral, Gemma, Qwen) as a within-family control against their Instruct counterparts
Five system-prompt defenses (best one summarized quantitatively) as a prompt-level mitigation baseline against the structured-dissent (single dissenter) mitigation
Three adaptive-attacker strategies tested against the dissenter-rescue mitigation
